title: Task 6: Deepen validation for first-error localization

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: Task 6 sounds simple on paper: Make label validation care more about the intended first-bad-step semantics, not just type/range checks.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: And the reason it matters is straightforward: Phase 0 emphasized first-error localization as a core supervision target. Current validation mostly checks that `bad_step` exists and is in range. That is necessary, but not enough.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: What I like here is that the implementation does not chase a grand redesign. It tightens one seam in the pipeline.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. The implemented result for Task 6 is now marked done in the plan, with concrete verification commands recorded for the repo.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [gasp] So what changes downstream because Task 6 is already implemented?

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Rule-aware localization validation makes bad_step supervision closer to the first-error signal that later evaluation actually depends on.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: That means the value is not just local correctness. It is leverage for everything that comes after.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Once labels are semantically sharper, saved build manifests become much more informative for comparing dataset versions.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [chuckle] This is very home-lab coded: small patch, big increase in trust.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Yes. It follows the same discipline across the whole plan: keep the architecture modest, but make the data and labels harder to fool.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: And because Task 6 is complete, the listener can treat this as a concrete repo change rather than a hypothetical roadmap item.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. This episode is not selling a future intention. It is documenting an implemented improvement and the next layer of value it unlocks.
